title: Sets
description: C has no built-in set type — use boolean arrays and bit manipulation to implement set operations efficiently for interview problems.

sections.title: Boolean array as a set
sections.explanation: The simplest set in C: a boolean (int) array where `set[x] = 1` means x is in the set. Works perfectly when values are bounded integers. O(1) add, remove, and membership check. This is the go-to approach for "has duplicate" and "visited" problems.

exercise.prompt: Create a boolean set array. Add values 3, 7, and 15. Check membership of 7 (print "found") and 9 (print "not found"). Then remove 7 and verify it's gone.

validation.message: 7 is found, 9 is not found, then 7 is removed
Use array assignment for add/remove and direct access for membership check

exercise.hint: set[x] = 1 to add, set[x] = 0 to remove. Check with if(set[x]) or if(!set[x]).

sections.title: Finding duplicates with a set
sections.explanation: Classic interview pattern: iterate through an array, check if each element was seen before using a boolean set. If `seen[x]` is already 1, it's a duplicate. This is O(n) time with O(max_val) space — the C equivalent of using a HashSet.

exercise.prompt: Find and print all duplicate values in {4, 2, 7, 2, 8, 4, 9} using a seen array. Print each duplicate once when first re-encountered.

validation.message: Use a seen array to detect duplicates on second occurrence

exercise.hint: Check if seen[nums[i]] is already 1 before setting it. Duplicates are 2 and 4.

sections.title: Bit manipulation as a set
sections.explanation: An `unsigned int` gives you a 32-element set in a single variable. **Add**: `set |= (1 << x)`. **Remove**: `set &= ~(1 << x)`. **Check**: `set & (1 << x)`. This is ultra-compact and fast — perfect for subsets, combinations, and problems with up to 32 elements.
sections.example:
unsigned int set = 0;
set |= (1 << 3);  // add 3
set |= (1 << 7);  // add 7
set |= (1 << 3);  // add 3 again (no change)

if (set & (1 << 3)) printf("3 in set\n");   // yes
if (!(set & (1 << 5))) printf("5 not in set\n"); // yes

set &= ~(1 << 3); // remove 3
if (!(set & (1 << 3))) printf("3 removed\n"); // yes

exercise.prompt: Use a bitmask to create a set. Add elements 1, 4, and 6. Check if 4 is present (print "yes"), check if 5 is present (print "no"). Remove 4 and verify (print "removed").

validation.message: 4 is in set, 5 is not, then 4 is removed
Use bit OR to add, AND with complement to remove, AND to check membership

exercise.hint: Add with |= (1 << x), check with & (1 << x), remove with &= ~(1 << x).

sections.title: Set union, intersection, difference
sections.explanation: With bitmask sets, set operations are single bitwise operations. **Union**: `a | b`. **Intersection**: `a & b`. **Difference**: `a & ~b`. **Subset check**: `(a & b) == a`. These are O(1) and extremely useful for subset enumeration problems.

exercise.prompt: Create two bitmask sets: A = {1, 3, 5} and B = {3, 5, 7}. Compute and print the intersection elements, then the union elements, each space-separated on their own line.

validation.message: Use & for intersection, | for union, and bit shifts for element encoding

exercise.hint: Intersection: a & b. Union: a | b. Iterate bits 0-7 and print those set.
